original_abstract: GROMACS is a widely used package for biomolecular simulation, and over the last two decades it has evolved from small-scale efﬁciency to advanced hetero- geneous acceleration and multi-level parallelism targeting some of the largest supercomputers in the world. Here, we describe some of the ways we have been able to realize this through the use of parallelization on all levels, combined with a constant focus on absolute performance. Release 4.6 of GROMACS uses SIMD acceleration on a wide range of architectures, GPU ofﬂoading acceleration, and both OpenMP and MPI parallelism within and between nodes, respectively. The recent work on acceleration made it necessary to revisit the fundamental algorithms of molecular simulation, including the concept of neighborsearching, and we discuss the present and future challenges we see for exascale simulation - in particular a very ﬁne-grained task parallelism. We also discuss the software management, code peer review and continuous integration testing required for a project of this complexity. 2
humanized_abstract: GROMACS is a program package designed for biomolecular simulation. During the last two decades GROMACS has evolved from being an efficiency tool for small simulations to a heterogeneous system that is also capable of simulating complex systems on the largest high performance computing systems available. In this talk we will discuss some of the concepts that have been employed in order to enable parallelization at every level of the code and, in conjunction with high performance on serial portions of the code, to maintain a high level of performance in the code. We will also provide a short overview of the code. The work done during the last year or so in the area of offloading to the GPU has necessitated a rethink of several basic aspects of the simulation algorithm, not least the neighborsearching part of the code, and we will also give some ideas about the future challenges we will be facing when approaching exascale simulations and what type of parallelism will be suitable for such simulations. Finally, we will also talk a bit about the issues of software management, and the procedures we have to go through in order to get the code reviewed and tested on all the many platforms that are supported by the current release, version 4.6, of GROMACS. 2
